content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Contrastive Prompting: Reducing Social Bias in Large Language Models via Dynamic Contextualization",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often reproduce or amplify social biases and stereotypes in their outputs, especially when prompted in open-ended ways. Current prompting strategies achieve only marginal or inconsistent debiasing effects.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive applications, mitigating bias in generated content is critical for fairness and societal trust. Prior work mostly relies on post-hoc filtering or static prompt engineering, which either harms utility or fails to generalize. A method that adaptively mitigates bias while retaining the original task intent is urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Contrastive Prompting' (CCP), a dynamic prompting framework that augments user inputs with on-the-fly generated counterfactual contexts. For each input prompt, CCP generates paired, minimally-differing prompts that swap demographic attributes (e.g., gender, ethnicity, age) using a lightweight attribute-editing model. The LLM is then prompted with both the original and counterfactual prompts, and output probabilities are explicitly contrasted to enforce demographic invariance via constrained decoding or prompt-level regularization. This approach requires no retraining and can be applied to any black-box LLM.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CCP on standard bias benchmarks (e.g., StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, WinoBias) and compare against direct prompting, instruction tuning, and self-debiasing baselines. Bias metrics (e.g., stereotype score, bias amplification) and utility metrics (e.g., accuracy, perplexity) are reported. Human evaluation will assess fluency and fairness of outputs."
}
